name: Simbi
description: Simbi are a diverse group of water spirits in Kongo mythology, often associated with bodies of water like rivers, springs, and lakes. They are considered powerful intermediaries between the human world and the spirit realm, possessing the ability to influence both the physical and spiritual well-being of individuals and communities. Some Simbi are benevolent, offering protection, healing, and prosperity, while others can be mischievous or even malevolent, causing misfortune and illness. Their origins are often shrouded in mystery, with some believed to be ancestral spirits who have ascended to a higher plane of existence. Simbi are frequently invoked in rituals and ceremonies, where offerings are made to appease them and seek their favor. They are also seen as guardians of sacred groves and natural resources, emphasizing the importance of respecting the environment. The specific roles and attributes of Simbi vary depending on the region and local traditions, reflecting the diverse cultural landscape of the Kongo people. These spirits are crucial to understanding the Kongo worldview, which emphasizes the interconnectedness of the living and the dead, the natural and the supernatural.
subtitle: Spirits of water and the wilderness

keyMyths.title: The Simbi and the Fisherman
keyMyths.description: A fisherman makes a pact with a Simbi for a bountiful catch, but fails to uphold his end of the bargain, facing the Simbi's wrath.

keyMyths.title: Simbi Healing Ritual
keyMyths.description: A sick villager is healed through a ritual invoking the Simbi, who guide the healer to the correct herbs and practices.

keyMyths.title: The Simbi and the Lost Child
keyMyths.description: A lost child is guided back to their village by a benevolent Simbi, who protects them from danger in the forest.
